walkthrough.title: Gather the Deck
walkthrough.text: Let's get Phase 10 set up. Grab the deck of 108 cards. You will see numbered cards from one through twelve in four different colors, plus some special cards. Give the whole deck a thorough shuffle.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: Now deal ten cards face down to each player. Everyone picks up their cards and keeps them hidden from other players. Ten cards is a big hand, so fan them out so you can see everything.

walkthrough.title: Create the Draw and Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: Place the remaining cards face down in the center of the table. This is your draw pile. Flip the top card face up next to it to start the discard pile. Make sure everyone can reach both piles easily.

walkthrough.title: Track Your Phases
walkthrough.text: Every player starts on Phase one. You might want to keep a phase reference card nearby so everyone can see what each phase requires. You can also just write down which phase each player is on. That is all for setup. Let's learn how to play.

summary.title: Gather the Deck
bullets: 108 cards total: 96 numbered (1-12 in 4 colors), 4 Skips, 8 Wilds
Shuffle thoroughly

summary.title: Deal the Cards
bullets: Deal 10 cards face-down to each player
Keep hand hidden from opponents

summary.title: Create the Draw and Discard Piles
bullets: Remaining cards → face-down draw pile in center
Flip top card face-up → discard pile

summary.title: Track Your Phases
bullets: All players start on Phase 1
Keep phase reference card or written tracker visible

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Phase 10 is a rummy-style card game where you race to complete ten specific phases in order. Each phase requires a different combination of cards, like sets of matching numbers or runs of consecutive numbers. The first player to complete all ten phases wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Draw
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you always start by drawing one card. You can take the top card from the draw pile or the top card from the discard pile. Drawing from the discard pile is great when that card directly helps you complete your current phase.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Lay Down Your Phase
walkthrough.text: After drawing, if you have all the cards needed for your current phase, you may lay them face up in front of you. This is optional on any turn, but you can only lay down your phase once per hand. You must lay down the complete phase all at once. You cannot lay down part of it now and finish later.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Hit
walkthrough.text: After you have laid down your phase, you may add cards from your hand onto any completed phase on the table. This is called hitting. You can hit on your own phase or on other players' phases. For runs, you can extend either end. For sets, you add matching numbers. Hitting helps you get rid of cards faster.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Discard
walkthrough.text: You must always discard, even if you only have one card left. If that last discard empties your hand, you have gone out and the round ends immediately.

walkthrough.title: The Ten Phases
walkthrough.text: Here are all ten phases. Phase one is two sets of three. Phase two is one set of three plus one run of four. Phase three is one set of four plus one run of four. Phase four is one run of seven. Phase five is one run of eight. Phase six is one run of nine. Phase seven is two sets of four. Phase eight is seven cards of one color. Phase nine is one set of five plus one set of two. Phase ten is one set of five plus one set of three.

walkthrough.title: Sets, Runs, and Colors
walkthrough.text: Let's clarify three key terms. A set is a group of cards with the same number, and color does not matter for sets. A run is a sequence of consecutive numbers like three, four, five, six, and again color does not matter. For Phase eight, you need seven cards of the same color, and the numbers do not matter for that one.

walkthrough.title: Wild and Skip Cards
walkthrough.text: Wild cards are incredibly powerful. A Wild can substitute for any number or any color, and you can use multiple Wilds in one phase. Skip cards work differently. When you discard a Skip card, the next player loses their turn entirely. Skip cards cannot be picked up from the discard pile and cannot be used as part of any phase.

walkthrough.title: End of a Hand
walkthrough.text: A hand ends when any player discards their last card and goes out. Everyone else gets stuck with cards in their hand, and those cards become penalty points. Cards one through nine are worth five points each. Cards ten through twelve are worth ten points each. Skip cards are fifteen points and Wild cards are a painful twenty-five points each.

walkthrough.title: Advancing Phases and Winning
walkthrough.text: If you completed your phase this hand, you advance to the next phase for the next hand. If you did not complete your phase, you stay on the same one and try again. Only one phase can be completed per hand. The game ends when someone completes Phase ten and goes out in the same hand. If multiple players finish Phase ten in the same hand, the player with the lowest total penalty score wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Rummy-style race through 10 specific phases
Complete phases in order, 1 per hand
First to complete Phase 10 wins

bullets: Optional: lay complete phase face-up in front of you
Must lay entire phase at once, not partial
Only 1 phase per hand

summary.title: Turn Structure: Hit
bullets: After laying your phase, add cards to any completed phase on table
Extend runs on either end, add matching numbers to sets
Helps dump cards from your hand

summary.title: Turn Structure: Discard
bullets: End turn by placing 1 card on discard pile
Must always discard — if hand empties, you go out and hand ends

Lower penalty score is better

Failed phase → retry same phase next hand
Game ends when someone completes Phase 10 and goes out
Tie on Phase 10 → lowest total penalty score wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Phase 10
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a scripted hand with four players. Everyone is starting on Phase one, which requires two sets of three. Each player has been dealt ten cards. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Check Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what Phase one needs: two sets of three matching numbers. Scan your hand for any pairs or triples. If you already have a pair of sevens and a pair of fours, you are only two cards away from completing the phase. Start thinking about which numbers to collect.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Smart Draw
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has two fours and two nines in hand. They check the discard pile and see a four sitting right on top. Player 1 grabs that four from the discard pile because it completes a set of three fours. Then they discard a card they do not need. Great start.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Good Move
walkthrough.text: Drawing from the discard pile when the card directly helps your phase is almost always the right call. You know exactly what you are getting, versus a random draw from the deck. When the discard helps, take it.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Draw and Plan
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws from the draw pile and gets a Wild card. Wilds can be any number, so Player 2 tucks it into their hand and starts planning. They already have two eights, so that Wild can be the third eight. Now they just need one more set of three.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: The Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has a messy hand with lots of different numbers but no pairs. Instead of focusing on building two sets, they notice a long sequence of five, six, seven, eight and start saving those cards for a run. But Phase one does not need runs. It needs two sets of three.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Focus on Your Current Phase
walkthrough.text: Never collect cards for a future phase when you have not completed your current one. Player 3 is wasting turns saving run cards when they need sets. Every card held for a future phase is a card that is not helping you right now. Complete your current phase first, then worry about what comes next.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Turn: Good Foundation
walkthrough.text: Player 4 draws from the deck and gets a three. They already have two threes, so now they have a complete set of three threes. They are halfway to laying down Phase one. Player 4 discards a lone twelve to reduce penalty risk.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Lays Down Phase One
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player 1 has collected three fours and three nines. They lay down both sets face up on the table. Phase one is complete! Now Player 1 can start hitting on anyone's completed phases to dump cards from their hand.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 3 finally has a pair of sixes and draws a Wild. Instead of using the Wild to complete a set of three sixes, they hold the Wild card, thinking they should save it for a harder phase later. Meanwhile, they cannot lay down their phase and cards keep piling up in their hand.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Wilds to Complete Your Phase Now
walkthrough.text: Do not hoard Wild cards for future phases. Completing your current phase is always the priority. A Wild sitting in your hand earning penalty points is far worse than a Wild used to finish Phase one. Use Wilds when they help you lay down your phase this hand. You will find more Wilds in later hands.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Goes Out
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has been hitting cards onto their own sets and other players' phases. Their hand is almost empty. They discard their last card and go out. The hand is over. Players 2 and 4 completed their phases earlier, so they advance to Phase two. Player 3 is stuck on Phase one because they never laid it down.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have played through your first hand of Phase 10. You saw how to draw wisely, lay down phases, hit on completed phases, and go out. You also watched Player 3 learn two important lessons: always focus on your current phase and use Wilds to complete it.

walkthrough.title: Before You Deal Again
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on managing your hand and using special cards.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all ten phases and card types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Watched Player 3 learn: focus on current phase, use Wilds now

summary.title: Before You Deal Again
bullets: Check out General Tips → hand management and special card strategy
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for all 10 phases
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Focus on Your Current Phase
walkthrough.text: The single most important rule in Phase 10 is to focus entirely on your current phase. Do not hold cards for a future phase. Do not plan three phases ahead. Every card in your hand should either be working toward your current phase or be something you are about to discard. Players who try to collect for Phase five while stuck on Phase three fall behind fast.

walkthrough.title: Draw from the Discard Pile Wisely
walkthrough.text: The discard pile is public information. When the top card directly helps your phase, take it. But be careful about tipping off your opponents. If you grab a six from the discard, everyone at the table now knows you are collecting sixes. Sometimes a blind draw from the deck is better for keeping your plans hidden, especially in the later phases.

walkthrough.title: Use Wild Cards Effectively
walkthrough.text: Wild cards are the most valuable cards in the game, but only if you use them. Do not hoard Wilds hoping for a perfect moment. Use them to complete your current phase as soon as possible. The exception is when you are very close to completing a phase without the Wild. In that case, holding it one more turn is fine. But never sit on a Wild for three or four turns while falling behind.

walkthrough.title: Use Skip Cards Strategically
walkthrough.text: Skip cards are pure disruption. The best time to use a Skip is against the player who is closest to completing their phase or who is furthest ahead in the game. Skipping a player who just needs one more card to lay down their phase can buy you a crucial extra turn. Remember that Skip cards are worth fifteen penalty points if you get stuck with them, so do not hold them too long.

walkthrough.title: Minimize Penalty Points
walkthrough.text: When it becomes clear you will not complete your phase this hand, switch strategies immediately. Start discarding your highest-value cards first. Tens, elevens, and twelves are ten points each. Wild cards are twenty-five points. Get rid of these expensive cards as fast as possible. Low cards one through nine are only five points each, so they are the least painful to hold.

walkthrough.title: Go Out Quickly After Laying Down
walkthrough.text: Once your phase is on the table, your only goal is to empty your hand. Hit cards onto any completed phase on the table, yours or opponents'. Do not be picky about where cards go. Every card you dump reduces your penalty risk and brings you closer to ending the hand. The player who goes out forces everyone else to take penalties.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what your opponents are picking up from the discard pile and what they are discarding. If someone grabs a seven, they probably need sevens. If someone keeps discarding high cards, they might be close to completing their phase and dumping hand weight. Use this information to decide when to use Skip cards and what cards are safe to discard.

walkthrough.title: Pace Awareness
walkthrough.text: Keep track of which phase each player is on. If you are on Phase three and everyone else is on Phase five or six, you need to prioritize speed over efficiency. Complete your phase with Wilds if necessary and do not worry about penalty points. Conversely, if you are ahead of the pack, you can afford to play a bit more carefully and conserve your Wilds.

summary.title: Focus on Your Current Phase
bullets: Every card should work toward your current phase
Don't plan ahead until current phase is complete
Collecting for future phases = falling behind

summary.title: Draw from the Discard Pile Wisely
bullets: Take discard when it directly helps your phase
Be aware: grabbing a specific card reveals your plan
Sometimes blind deck draw is better for secrecy

summary.title: Use Wild Cards Effectively
bullets: Use Wilds to complete your current phase — don't hoard
Only hold a Wild briefly if you're about to complete without it
Sitting on Wilds for 3-4 turns = falling behind + 25-pt penalty risk

summary.title: Use Skip Cards Strategically
bullets: Target the leader or the player closest to laying down
Skip = 15 penalty points, so discard before hand ends
Timing a Skip can buy you a crucial extra turn

summary.title: Minimize Penalty Points
bullets: If you can't complete your phase, dump high-value cards first
10-12 = 10 pts, Skip = 15, Wild = 25 — discard these ASAP
Low cards (1-9) = only 5 pts each

summary.title: Go Out Quickly After Laying Down
bullets: After phase is down, hit cards onto ANY completed phase
Don't be picky — dump cards fast to end the hand
Going out forces opponents to take penalty points

summary.title: Watch Your Opponents
bullets: Track what opponents draw from discard pile
High-card discards often signal phase completion is close
Use opponent reads to time Skips and safe discards

summary.title: Pace Awareness
bullets: Track which phase everyone is on
Behind? Prioritize speed — use Wilds freely
Ahead? Play more carefully, conserve special cards

walkthrough.title: Middle Numbers for Runs
walkthrough.text: When you reach the run phases, specifically Phases four through six, prioritize middle numbers like five, six, seven, and eight. These numbers can extend in either direction, giving you maximum flexibility. Edge numbers like one or twelve can only connect on one side, making them far less useful for building long runs.

walkthrough.title: Wild Card Conservation for Hard Phases
walkthrough.text: Phases four through six require runs of seven, eight, and nine cards. These are the hardest phases in the game because consecutive numbers are difficult to collect. If you are breezing through the set phases, consider saving a Wild for these run phases. But only do this if you can still complete your current phase quickly without it.

walkthrough.title: Phase Eight: The Color Phase
walkthrough.text: This is a unique challenge because color normally does not matter in Phase 10. Start paying attention to which color appears most in your hand early. Do not commit to a color until you have at least three or four of the same one. Wild cards are especially powerful here since they can be any color.

walkthrough.title: Skip Card Timing
walkthrough.text: The best moment to play a Skip card is when an opponent is about to lay down their phase or go out. Watch for players who suddenly start discarding high cards or who have very few cards left. Skipping them at the right moment can cost them an entire hand's worth of progress. In a close race, one well-timed Skip can decide the game.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Discarding
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what your opponents need. If the player to your left is collecting fives, do not discard a five for them to grab from the discard pile. Sometimes holding a card that does not help you is worth it if discarding it would help an opponent complete their phase. This is especially important when opponents are on the same phase as you or ahead of you.

walkthrough.title: Hitting Strategy
walkthrough.text: After laying down your phase, think about where to hit your remaining cards. You can add cards to any completed phase on the table, not just your own. This is critical for going out quickly. If you have a nine and someone has a run of six through twelve on the table, that nine is useless there. But if someone has a set of nines, you can hit your nine onto it. Scan all completed phases for opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: When you reach Phases nine and ten, every hand matters enormously. These phases require large sets, which means Wild cards are your best friend. Do not waste turns trying to collect naturally when a Wild can close the gap. In the final phases, speed beats efficiency. A player who completes Phase ten with a high penalty score still beats someone stuck on Phase eight.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table
walkthrough.text: Advanced players track more than just their own hand. Count how many of each number have been played to the table in completed phases. If three nines are already visible in laid-down phases, the odds of drawing the fourth nine from the deck are low. This information helps you decide whether to commit to a set or pivot to different numbers. The more you track what is already on the table, the smarter your collection choices become.

summary.title: Middle Numbers for Runs
bullets: Phases 4-6 (long runs): prioritize 5, 6, 7, 8
Middle numbers extend in both directions
Edge numbers (1, 12) only connect on one side

summary.title: Wild Card Conservation for Hard Phases
bullets: Phases 4-6 (runs of 7-9) are the hardest phases
Consider saving Wilds if you can complete current phase without one
Only conserve if it doesn't cost you the current hand

summary.title: Phase Eight: The Color Phase
bullets: Need 7 cards of 1 color — only phase where color matters
Don't commit until you have 3-4 of one color
Wilds are any color — extremely powerful here

summary.title: Skip Card Timing
bullets: Skip opponents about to lay down or go out
Watch for sudden high-card discards = phase almost done
One well-timed Skip can decide a close game

summary.title: Defensive Discarding
bullets: Don't feed opponents cards they need
Sometimes hold an unhelpful card to deny opponents
Especially important against players on same or higher phase

summary.title: Hitting Strategy
bullets: After phase is down, scan ALL completed phases for hit targets
Can hit on opponents' phases, not just your own
Key to going out fast and ending the hand

summary.title: Endgame Acceleration
bullets: Phases 9-10: speed beats efficiency
Use Wilds aggressively — don't wait for natural draws
Completing Phase 10 with high penalty still beats being stuck

summary.title: Reading the Table
bullets: Count numbers already in completed phases on table
Low remaining copies = low odds of drawing them
Use table reads to decide: commit to set or pivot

entries.definition: A group of cards with the same number. Color does not matter for sets. For example, three cards all showing the number 7 form a set of three.
A sequence of consecutively numbered cards. Color does not matter for runs. For example, 3-4-5-6 is a run of four.
A special card that can substitute for any number or color. Multiple Wilds can be used in the same phase. Worth 25 penalty points if left in hand.
A special card that, when discarded, causes the next player to lose their entire turn. Cannot be picked up from the discard pile and cannot be used as part of any phase. Worth 15 penalty points.
Two sets of three. Collect two groups of three matching numbers.
One set of three plus one run of four. Collect three matching numbers and four consecutive numbers.
One set of four plus one run of four. Collect four matching numbers and four consecutive numbers.
One run of seven. Collect seven consecutively numbered cards.
One run of eight. Collect eight consecutively numbered cards.
One run of nine. Collect nine consecutively numbered cards.
Two sets of four. Collect two groups of four matching numbers.
Seven cards of one color. The only phase where color matters and numbers do not.
One set of five plus one set of two. Collect five matching numbers and two matching numbers.
One set of five plus one set of three. Collect five matching numbers and three matching numbers. Completing this and going out wins the game.

After you have laid down your phase, you may add cards from your hand to any completed phase on the table. You can extend runs on either end or add matching numbers to sets. Hitting helps you empty your hand faster.
When a player discards their last card, they go out and the hand ends immediately. All other players score penalty points for cards remaining in their hand.
The face-down deck of remaining cards in the center of the table. You draw from here when the discard pile does not help you.
The face-up pile where players place one card at the end of each turn. The top card can be drawn by the next player, except Skip cards which cannot be taken from the discard pile.

Points scored for cards left in hand when someone goes out. Cards 1-9 are worth 5 points each, cards 10-12 are worth 10 points each, Skip cards are 15 points, and Wild cards are 25 points. Lower is better.
